text: The surgeon observes that the large ovarian cyst extends up to the umbilicus and prepares to gain access through Palmer's point.
The video shows the costal margin, which is the lower edge of the rib cage, as part of the anatomical landmarks for surgical access.
The video shows the left and right subcostal regions, highlighting their anatomical positions beneath the rib cage.
The camera view shows the midclavicular line, which is a reference point for surgical access.
The surgeon identifies the midclavicular line and indicates a modification to the standard approach at this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon identifies the original Palmer's point, located at the midclavicular line just below the costal margin, and marks a point two centimeters above it.
The surgeon creates a small, two-millimeter stab wound over the inferior crease of the umbilicus.
The surgeon inserts the Veress needle into the abdominal wall, stretching it downward and directing the needle towards the stomach for insufflation.
The surgeon holds a syringe filled with saline solution, preparing to use it for irrigation or testing purposes during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs the irrigation test, demonstrating the free flow of saline through the varus needle inserted above the umbilicus.
The surgeon performs a suction test, with no fluid being extracted from the surgical site.
The surgeon attaches the tubing of the insufflator, causing the hanging drop to be visibly sucked into the tubing.
The tubing of the insufflator is shown, likely connected to the laparoscopic equipment for insufflation during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a Striker insufflator with an inbuilt CO₂ warmer to maintain optimal insufflation conditions during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon initiates insufflation, introducing CO₂ gas into the abdominal cavity to create pneumoperitoneum for better visualization during the appendectomy.
The digital output of the insufflator displays the pre-check pressure set at fifteen, with axial pressure being adjusted slowly.
The digital display shows the axial pressure gradually increasing during the insufflation process.
The insufflator is stopped upon reaching fifteen mmHg pressure. The incision is enlarged to eleven mm, and the trocar is introduced, followed by the insertion of the telescope into the surgical site.

The surgeon positions the index finger on the right infundibulopelvic ligament and the thumb on the umbilicus, forming a diamond shape with the hand. The arc is drawn towards the right infundibulopelvic ligament.

The surgical table is adjusted to a 30-degree head-down position. The first arc is measured at 18 centimeters and the second arc at 24 centimeters. The telescope is positioned at Palmer's point, with the primary port located 7.5 centimeters laterally. The surgeon uses both hands to maintain triangulation and achieve a 60-degree manipulation angle, followed by the placement of the second port.
The surgeon introduces a 5mm working port, positioned 7.5 centimeters lateral and below the umbilicus, into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a 5mm port, positioned laterally and below the umbilicus, as part of the laparoscopic setup for the appendectomy procedure.
The assistant gynecologist introduces a trocar through the newly established port, preparing for further surgical access.

The surgeon uses an aspiration needle to puncture the cyst, collecting initial few milliliters of cystic fluid into a syringe for FNAC. The trocar is visible inside, with another trocar positioned on the opposite side.
The surgeon uses a needle to puncture the cyst, allowing for the aspiration of cystic fluid.
The surgeon punctures the cyst using an aspiration needle, allowing cystic fluid to be collected into a syringe for further analysis.
The surgeon collects fluid from the cyst into a syringe for cytological examination, using an aspiration needle inserted through the trocar.
The surgeon uses suction to remove the fluid from the surgical site, following the collection of cystic fluid into a syringe for cytological examination.
The surgeon applies entromedial traction, likely manipulating anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a salpingo-oophorectomy, visually cutting and removing the fallopian tube and ovary.
The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to the tissue, manipulating the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform the procedure, with the choice of instrument left to the surgeon's discretion.
The surgeon performs a right-sided salpingo-oophorectomy, visually identifying and removing the fallopian tube and ovary from the surrounding pelvic structures.
The surgeon begins to insert a sponge into the posterior fornix, using gentle pressure to position it within the anatomical space.
The assistant pushes a sponge into the posterior fornix, providing clear visualization of the anatomical area during the procedure.
The assistant pushes the sponge into the posterior fornix, creating a visible bulge in the tissue.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the posterior fornix approximately two to three centimeters below the arc of the uterosacral ligament.
The surgeon performs a posterior colpotomy, creating an incision in the posterior fornix area.
The assistant uses a screwing motion to insert the sponge into the abdominal cavity through the colpotomy incision.
The surgeon places a claw forceps beside the sponge within the abdominal cavity.
A claw forceps is introduced into the surgical field, visible as it approaches the area of operation.
The surgeon uses forceps to hold the cysts, securing them for further manipulation.
The assistant uses claw forceps to grasp and manipulate tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses claw forceps to extract the ovary, tube, and cysts through the vaginal colpotomy wound, visibly pulling these structures out.
The surgeon identifies the omentum and holds the appendix using the same port, preparing to perform an appendectomy without requiring an additional port.
The surgeon retracts the appendix upward and uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the major portion of the appendix from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device with its Teflon jaw positioned towards the cecum to slowly separate the major appendix from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon ensures that no current or vibration passes over the appendix to prevent thermal injury, revealing the entire major appendix free from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon reaches the tinea coli and cecum during the appendectomy, indicating the anatomical location of the appendix in relation to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon prepares to secure the appendix using an extracorporeal Visras knot, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a grasper to catch the appendix and maneuvers it behind surrounding structures, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon positions the appendix at its base and ties a knot to secure it, using the extracorporeal knotting technique.
The surgeon tightens the extracorporeal knot around the base of the appendix, ensuring secure ligation of the structure.
The surgeon completes the appendectomy procedure, with the appendix secured and the knot tied at its base.
The surgeon cuts the suture and uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the appendix, leaving a six-millimeter margin from the knot. The appendicular stump is sealed to prevent leakage.
The surgeon uses a stone holding forceps to manipulate the appendix, positioning it behind surrounding structures.
The surgeon inserts stone holding forceps through the posterior fornix to grasp the base of the appendix with spoon forceps, ensuring no spillage as the appendix is extracted.
